subject: Taste of Bellwood 2025 - planning kickoff, dates, budget ask
body_clean:
Mayor, kicking off Taste of Bellwood 2025 planning so we lock the calendar before vendors book elsewhere. I'm proposing the weekend of Friday August 15 through Sunday August 17, same footprint as last year on St. Charles Rd between 25th Ave and Bohland Ave, with the main stage at the 25th Ave end and the beer garden tented in the municipal lot behind 3200 Washington Blvd. That's roughly 1,400 linear feet of road closure plus the lot.

High-level budget: last year we ran $112,400 all-in and netted about $18,600 after vendor fees and the sponsor money. This year I'm building to $128,000 because stage rental jumped (Midwest Staging quoted $9,200 vs. $7,400 in 2024), portable restrooms are up about 14 percent, and police/fire OT is the wildcard I need help scoping. Revenue plan: 28 to 32 food vendors at $850 a booth, 12 craft/retail at $450, a title sponsor we're chasing at $25,000, and beer garden sales which netted $31,000 last year.

The critical-path items that need YOUR sign-off or a department handoff in the next two weeks: (1) the road-closure resolution has to hit the April 7 Village Board agenda or we miss the window, (2) police security/OT detail scoping with Ofc. Banks, (3) fire EMS standby and the fire-lane plan, and (4) an IDOT staging question because I want to stage vendor trucks on the Mannheim Rd frontage road this year and that's a state route. Can we get a planning huddle on the calendar? I'll bring the site map and the draft P&L.

subject: Taste of Bellwood 2025 - green light, get the closure on April 7
body_clean:
Kevin, this is good work and I'm in on August 15-17. Let's make this the best one yet - it's the village's signature event and the Board likes seeing it net positive.

Get the road-closure resolution drafted with Yolanda and on the April 7 agenda; do not let that slip, because the next meeting isn't until the 21st and that compresses everything downstream. Loop Karen in on the budget so finance has eyes on the $128,000 before it shows up as a surprise - I want the P&L to show the sponsor money as contingent, not booked, until we have a signed commitment.

On the items you flagged: yes to scoping police OT with Reggie early, because last year the detail came in $4,000 over what we projected and the Board noticed. Fire standby - get the Shift Commander a footprint map so they can mark the fire lanes before we sell booths into spaces we'll have to claw back. And the Mannheim Rd staging idea makes me nervous; that's IDOT's jurisdiction and they are slow, so if you want it for August you need to open that conversation now, not June.

Keep me on the thread. Tina will help you herd the meeting onto everyone's calendar.

subject: Security detail and OT estimate for Taste closure
body_clean:
Mayor, Kevin, here's my first cut on the security and traffic plan for the August 15-17 closure.

Closure points: I'll need hard barricades and posted officers at St. Charles Rd and 25th Ave, St. Charles and Bohland, and the two mid-block alley crossings. The 25th Ave intersection is the problem child because it carries the detour for anybody coming off Washington Blvd, so I want a flagger there during load-in and load-out, not just barricades.

Staffing: I'm scoping two officers per shift for Friday evening (5pm-midnight), four per shift Saturday split across two shifts (11am-7pm, 7pm-1am), and three Sunday (noon-8pm). That's roughly 92 OT hours. At the current contractual OT rate of $68.50/hr fully loaded, that's about $6,300 in police OT alone, before the beer garden detail. If the Board wants a dedicated officer inside the beer garden each night per the liquor-license condition, add another 18 hours, about $1,230.

So plan on $7,500 to $7,800 for PD, which is up from the $5,900 we spent in 2024 - the contract raise is most of it. Two asks: confirm whether the beer garden runs all three nights or just Fri/Sat, because that swings the number, and get me the final booth map so I can confirm a 20-foot fire lane stays clear down the centerline. I do not want a repeat of 2024 when the funnel-cake trailer parked across the lane.

subject: EMS standby and fire-lane requirements - Taste footprint
body_clean:
Mayor, Kevin, Reggie - reviewed the draft footprint and I have non-negotiables before booths get sold.

Fire lane: NFPA 1 and our own adopted fire code require an unobstructed 20-foot emergency access lane down the centerline of St. Charles Rd for the full 1,400 feet, with no vendor encroachment, no guy-wires, no generator cords crossing at grade unless they're ramped and rated. Last year we measured 16 feet at the worst pinch point by the beer tent and that is a citation waiting to happen. Kevin, when you lay out the booth grid, mark the lane in a contrasting color and hold it.

EMS standby: for a crowd this size - you were estimating 9,000 to 12,000 over the weekend - I want a dedicated ALS ambulance and two-person crew staged at the 25th Ave end Friday 5pm-midnight, Saturday 11am-1am, and Sunday noon-8pm. That's a standby crew, so it's overtime backfill: roughly 41 hours of standby. At our backfill rate that's about $4,800. If the Board wants a bike-medic team working the crowd Saturday night, add a two-person team for the 7pm-1am window, about $1,400 more.

Two more items: the beer garden tent needs a fire-marshal occupancy sign-off and we'll need to verify the propane setups on the food line - any vendor running propane has to show me a current tank cert and keep a 2A:10BC extinguisher within 30 feet. Send me the vendor list as it firms up so I can pre-screen the propane operators.

subject: Trent's Smokehouse - headliner BBQ vendor interest for Taste
body_clean:
Hello Mayor Okonkwo and the Taste of Bellwood team. My name is Marcus Trent, I own Trent's Smokehouse out of Maywood, and your events coordinator Kevin reached out about us anchoring the food lineup this August. I wanted to introduce myself directly and lay out what we'd need so there are no surprises.

We're a full competition-grade BBQ operation - two 250-gallon offset smokers on a 28-foot gooseneck trailer, plus a 20-foot prep/serving tent. At a festival your size we typically move 2,500 to 3,200 plates over a weekend, so I'd want a double booth, a 30-foot run minimum, and importantly a corner or end-cap location because the smoke needs to vent away from the crowd and away from other tents. Power: I run off my own generator but I need it positioned so the cord doesn't cross your fire lane - I read that's a concern.

Logistics that matter to me: I need to roll the gooseneck in no later than 6am Friday for load-in because the smokers take six hours to come up to temp, and I can't break down until the trailer cools, so a hard midnight kick-out won't work for me on Sunday. I'd also need water access within about 75 feet for sanitation.

On money - I understand the booth fee is $850 for a single, so I'd assume $1,700 for the double. I'm willing to commit today if we can confirm the end-cap and the early load-in. Can we lock it? I turn down three festivals that weekend.

subject: Status roundup - Trent's locked, OT/EMS numbers, Mannheim staging still open
body_clean:
Mayor, consolidated status so everyone's working off one sheet.

Vendors: Trent's Smokehouse is our headliner - I'm putting them on the end-cap at the 25th Ave corner, which solves Marcus's smoke-venting need AND keeps the gooseneck off the centerline fire lane Frank's worried about. Double booth at $1,700, confirmed verbally, contract going out this week with a 6am Friday load-in window written in. We're at 24 food vendors committed, 8 more in the pipeline, and 9 craft. If we hit 30 food at $850 average plus Trent's premium, that's about $26,300 in food booth revenue.

Public safety: combining Reggie and Frank's numbers, I'm now carrying $7,800 police OT and $6,200 EMS/fire standby (including the Saturday bike-medic team), call it $14,000 for public safety, up from $9,400 in 2024. That's a real hit to the P&L and it's why the sponsor matters.

Beer garden: recommend all three nights to maximize the $31k revenue line, with the dedicated in-garden officer each night per the liquor condition - Reggie, that's the higher number you quoted, I'll own it.

Still open and worrying me: the Mannheim Rd staging permit with IDOT. I want to stage Trent's gooseneck and three other large rigs on the Mannheim frontage Thursday night so they're not circling residential streets at 5am Friday. I've drafted the application but IDOT District 1 wants a traffic-control plan stamped by an engineer. Tom - can you stamp a TCP for the frontage staging? Without it I don't think we get the permit in time.

subject: Suburban Bank & Trust - title sponsor proposal for Taste of Bellwood
body_clean:
Dear Mayor Okonkwo, I'm Denise Karpova, Community Relations Director for Suburban Bank & Trust. We've supported Taste of Bellwood at the $10,000 silver level the past two years and we'd like to step up to title sponsor for 2025. I want to put a concrete offer in front of you and your team.

Proposal: $25,000 title sponsorship. In return we'd want top billing - "Taste of Bellwood presented by Suburban Bank & Trust" on the main stage banner, the street banners at 25th Ave and Bohland, all printed programs and the village website event page, plus a 10-by-20 activation booth near the main stage where we'll do a kids' zone and a financial-literacy giveaway. We'd also like four mentions from the stage each night and our logo on the volunteer t-shirts.

Two conditions I have to be straight about: First, our marketing committee needs the signed agreement and final artwork specs by June 13 to hit our print and compliance deadlines - we cannot move that date. Second, our underwriting requires a certificate of insurance from the village naming Suburban Bank & Trust as additional insured, and confirmation that the event carries at least $2 million general liability. If the village can meet those two items, I'm authorized to commit the full $25,000 and cut a check within ten business days of signing.

Kevin mentioned the public-safety costs are up this year. We're glad the money helps where it's needed. Can your team turn around a draft agreement?

subject: IDOT Mannheim staging - TCP feasibility and timeline reality check
body_clean:
Kevin, Mayor - on the Mannheim Rd frontage staging and the IDOT permit, here's the engineering reality.

I can produce and stamp a traffic-control plan for staging four large rigs on the Mannheim frontage Thursday night into Friday morning. The TCP isn't the hard part - it's a temporary-occupancy layout with cones, advance warning signs, and a flagged ingress at the St. Charles Rd intersection. I can have a stamped TCP ready in about a week.

The hard part is IDOT District 1's process. Mannheim Rd (IL-72 corridor) is a state route, so this is a Highway Permit application, not something the village can approve on its own. District 1 quotes 30 to 45 business days on temporary-use permits, and they almost always come back once for revisions. We are at late May. Counting backward from August 14, we have just enough runway IF we submit the complete package - application, stamped TCP, certificate of insurance, and the closure resolution - by the first week of June. If it slips past mid-June, I would not bet on having the permit in hand.

My honest recommendation: file it now as the primary plan, but have Kevin build a fallback that stages the rigs entirely on village property - the municipal lot behind 3200 Washington plus the closed section of St. Charles itself after the Thursday-night barricade goes up. It's tighter and Marcus's 28-foot gooseneck will have to do a three-point turn, but it keeps us off IDOT's critical path. Let me know and I'll start the TCP today.

subject: Concerns about parking and noise from the August festival
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo, I live on the 200 block of Bohland Ave, half a block from where they close the street for Taste of Bellwood, and I'm writing before it's too late to do anything because last year was genuinely awful and nobody warned us.

The parking: when you close St. Charles Rd and turn the lot behind Village Hall into a beer garden, every single festival-goer parks on Bohland, Eastern, and Marshall. I could not get my own car into my own block all weekend, and an ambulance had a hard time getting to my neighbor Mr. Pawlak because cars were double-parked on both sides. People blocked my driveway twice and the non-emergency line told me it would be hours.

The noise: the main stage faces right up 25th Ave but the sound carries straight down to us, and last year the band was still going at 11:40pm Saturday. I'm 71, I have a grandson who stays with me, and there is no sleeping through it. I counted the village noise ordinance says amplified sound stops at 11pm in residential zones - is the festival exempt? Who decided that?

I'm not against the festival. I just want to know: will there be resident parking permits or a barricaded spot for those of us who live inside the closure ring, will the stage actually shut off at the legal hour, and is anyone keeping the fire lane clear on the side streets this time? I'd like a real answer, not a form letter.

subject: Your festival concerns - here is what we are doing
body_clean:
Dear Ms. Brzezinski, thank you for writing before the event instead of after, and I'm sorry last year left you boxed in - the ambulance access issue on Bohland is exactly the kind of thing that's unacceptable and I've made it a priority for the planning team. This is a real answer, not a form letter.

Three concrete changes for August 15-17, which I'm directing today:

Parking: we will create a resident-only zone on the inner blocks of Bohland, Eastern, and Marshall inside the closure ring. Kevin will mail hang-tag permits to every household on those blocks by August 1, and we'll post "Resident Permit Only" signs Thursday before load-in. Ofc. Banks's detail will tow double-parked and driveway-blocking vehicles on those streets, not refer it to a non-emergency queue - and the side-street fire lanes will be enforced, not just the main one.

Noise: you're right about the ordinance. Amplified sound in or adjacent to residential zones cuts at 11:00pm, and the festival is NOT exempt - last year's 11:40 run was a violation and it will not happen again. The stage manager will have a hard 10:45 last-song call and power down by 11:00, and I'm asking Kevin to angle the main speaker array and add delay towers so less sound travels down toward Bohland.

Access: a marked emergency lane will be kept open to your block for the full weekend, and EMS will have a staged ambulance at 25th Ave. If you ever can't get through, call the festival command post - Kevin will get you that direct number with your permit packet. I'll have Tina follow up so you have a name to reach.

subject: IDOT District 1 Highway Permit 1-2025-0884 - Mannheim Rd temporary staging
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo and Village of Bellwood, this is Raymond Holcomb with IDOT District 1 Permits regarding your application 1-2025-0884 for temporary commercial-vehicle staging on the Mannheim Rd frontage at St. Charles Rd, August 14-15.

We've completed first review. The application is substantially complete - the stamped traffic-control plan from your engineer Mr. Reyes meets our standards and the certificate of insurance naming the State of Illinois as additional insured is on file. However, before I can issue, I need two corrections and one commitment:

First, your TCP shows staging extending to within 40 feet of the St. Charles Rd intersection. District 1 standards require a minimum 75-foot intersection clearance on a route carrying Mannheim's volume for sight-line and turning-radius reasons. Mr. Reyes needs to revise and re-stamp; that's a minor edit but it has to come back stamped.

Second, the permit will carry a condition that the frontage be fully cleared and swept by 7:00am Friday August 15 - no rigs staged into the morning peak. Your application implied rigs would roll into the festival footprint "by 6am," and I need written confirmation the frontage is empty by 7.

Third, the permit fee is $1,450 and a refundable $5,000 restoration bond, payable before issuance. If you return the revised TCP and the fee/bond this week, I can have the permit issued by July 25 - comfortably ahead of your event. Miss this week and I can't guarantee the timeline given my review queue.

subject: Everything is locked - final P&L and go-list for Aug 15-17
body_clean:
Mayor, we are GO for August 15-17. Final status so you can walk into the Board meeting with clean numbers.

IDOT: Tom turned the revised TCP around in two days - he pulled the staging back to the 75-foot intersection clearance Holcomb required and added the 7am Friday sweep note. David cut the $1,450 fee and posted the $5,000 restoration bond. Permit 1-2025-0884 issued July 24. The Mannheim frontage staging is approved, so Trent's gooseneck and the three other large rigs stage Thursday night and roll into the footprint by 6am, frontage swept by 7. No more 5am circling on residential streets.

Sponsor: Suburban Bank & Trust signed at $25,000 title. We met Denise's June 13 deadline, finance issued the COI naming the bank as additional insured against the village's $2M GL policy, and the check cleared. It's now BOOKED revenue, not contingent.

Vendors: 31 food (Trent's on the 25th Ave end-cap, double at $1,700), 11 craft. Booth revenue $27,600. Beer garden running all three nights with the in-garden officer per the liquor condition.

Public safety: PD OT locked at $7,800, EMS/fire standby at $6,200 including the Saturday bike-medic team. Fire lane held at a true 20 feet on the centerline - Frank and I walked the grid.

Resident plan: Bohland/Eastern/Marshall permit hang-tags mailed, signs going up Thursday, Ms. Brzezinski has the command-post number, hard 11pm stage cutoff with delay towers.

Final P&L: $129,400 expense, $147,200 revenue, projected net $17,800. A hair under last year because of the OT, but the sponsor saved us. Thank you for clearing the path on all of it.
